evidence_text: Existing agentic coding benchmarks are described as limited in scope, often centered on bug fixing within a single PR and often lacking executable evaluation or scalable update mechanisms.
source_excerpt: "Existing agentic coding benchmarks, however, cover a limited task scope, e.g., bug fixing within a single pull request (PR), and often rely on non-executable evaluations or lack an automated approach for continually updating the evaluation coverage."
note:
This is the paper's stated characterization of prior benchmarks.
```
caveat:
This is the paper's stated characterization of prior benchmarks.
```

evidence_text: FeatureBench uses an execution-based evaluation protocol and a test-driven collection method that derives tasks from code repositories with minimal human effort.
source_excerpt: "To address such issues, we propose FeatureBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate agentic coding performance in end-to-end, feature-oriented software development. FeatureBench incorporates an execution-based evaluation protocol and a scalable test-driven method that automatically derives tasks from code repositories with minimal human effort."
note:
This states the benchmark's core evaluation and collection method.
```
caveat:
This states the benchmark's core evaluation and collection method.
```

evidence_text: The collection pipeline traces unit tests through a dependency graph to identify feature-level tasks that can span multiple commits and PRs while checking that other features still function after separation.
source_excerpt: "By tracing from unit tests along a dependency graph, our approach can identify feature-level coding tasks spanning multiple commits and PRs scattered across the development timeline, while ensuring the proper functioning of other features after the separation."
note:
This is the paper's stated extraction mechanism.
```
caveat:
This is the paper's stated extraction mechanism.
```

evidence_text: The first benchmark release contains 200 evaluation tasks and 3825 executable or verifiable environments drawn from 24 open-source repositories, with tasks created from May 2022 to September 2025.
source_excerpt: "Using this framework, we curated 200 challenging evaluation tasks and 3825 executable environments from 24 open-source repositories in the first version of our benchmark." "Using this pipeline, we have curated a benchmark with 200 evaluation instances and 3825 verifiable environments, created from May 2022 to September 2025, sourced from 24 real-world GitHub repositories in the first version of our benchmark."
note:
The source uses both "executable" and "verifiable" environment wording for the same benchmark inventory.
```
caveat:
The source uses both "executable" and "verifiable" environment wording for the same benchmark inventory.
```

evidence_text: Claude Opus 4.5 is reported at 74.4% resolved on SWE-bench but only 11.0% resolved on the full FeatureBench benchmark.
source_excerpt: "Empirical evaluation reveals that the state-of-the-art agentic model, such as Claude 4.5 Opus, which achieves a 74.4% resolved rate on SWE-bench, succeeds on only 11.0% of tasks." Table 2 lists "Claude Code Claude Opus 4.5" at "43.29 % Passed" and "11.0 % Resolved" on the Full set.
note:
This is a direct cross-benchmark performance comparison reported by the paper.
```
caveat:
This is a direct cross-benchmark performance comparison reported by the paper.
```

evidence_text: GPT-5.1-Codex with medium reasoning is reported to resolve 12.5% of tasks on the full FeatureBench set.
source_excerpt: "Using the Codex agent framewor, GPT-5.1-Codex (medium reasoning) successfully completes 12.5% of the task cases." Table 2 lists "Codex GPT-5.1-Codex ‡" at "12.5 % Resolved" on the Full set.
note:
The paper also reports 41.66% passed rate and 6.3M / 39k token I/O for this setting in Table 2.
```
caveat:
The paper also reports 41.66% passed rate and 6.3M / 39k token I/O for this setting in Table 2.
```

evidence_text: Compared with SWE-bench, FeatureBench L1 tasks are larger on average: 4818.0 vs 195.1 words in problem text, 790.2 vs 32.8 gold-solution lines, 15.7 vs 1.7 files, 29.2 vs 3 functions, and 62.7 vs 9.1 fail-to-pass test points.
source_excerpt: Table 3 reports for "SWE-bench" vs "Ours": "Problem Texts Length (Words) 195.1 4818.0", "Gold Solution # Lines 32.8 790.2", "# Files 1.7 15.7", "# Functions 3 29.2", and "Tests # Fail to pass (test points) 9.1 62.7".
note:
This comparison is specifically reported for "our FeatureBench (L1 set)."
```
caveat:
This comparison is specifically reported for "our FeatureBench (L1 set)."
```

evidence_text: On the subset restricted to repositories shared with SWE-bench, Claude Opus 4.5 is reported at 74.40% resolved on SWE-bench Verified versus 5.2% resolved on the FeatureBench subset.
source_excerpt: "Specifically, the most capable Claude Opus 4.5 only resolves 5.2% of the tasks in our FeatureBench subset in contrast to the 74.40% on the SWE-bench." Table 4 reports "Claude Opus 4.5" with "74.40" for SWE-bench Verified and "5.2" resolved for the FeatureBench subset.
note:
The paper presents this subset comparison as a fairer cross-benchmark alignment.
```
caveat:
The paper presents this subset comparison as a fairer cross-benchmark alignment.
```

evidence_text: Giving agents visible unit tests on the lite set substantially increased reported performance: Gemini-3-Pro-Preview rose from 10.0% to 60.0% resolved and GPT-5.1-Codex rose from 20.0% to 63.3% resolved.
source_excerpt: Table 7 reports under "Visible Unit Tests" that Gemini-3-Pro-Preview goes from the original lite-set baseline to "60.0 (+50.0)" resolved and GPT-5.1-Codex to "63.3 (+43.3)" resolved.
note:
This evidence is about the measured effect of exposing ground-truth tests.
```
caveat:
This evidence is about the measured effect of exposing ground-truth tests.
```

evidence_text: Removing explicit interface information reduced lite-set performance: Gemini-3-Pro-Preview fell to 3.3% resolved and GPT-5.1-Codex fell to 16.7% resolved.
source_excerpt: "As shown in Table 7, this removal leads to a marked decline in task success rates." Table 7 reports under "Without Interface" that Gemini-3-Pro-Preview is at "3.3 (-6.7)" resolved and GPT-5.1-Codex is at "16.7 (-3.3)" resolved.
note:
The source frames this as evidence that explicit interfaces matter for agent performance.
```
caveat:
The source frames this as evidence that explicit interfaces matter for agent performance.
```

evidence_text: Performance on manually revised lite-set prompts was reported as broadly consistent with performance on the original dataset.
source_excerpt: "As shown in Table 5, model performance on the manually revised subset is highly consistent with the original dataset." Table 5 reports Gemini-3-Pro-Preview at 10.0% resolved on both Original and Verified, and GPT-5.1-Codex at 16.7% vs 20.0% resolved.
note:
The evidence supports the paper's reliability claim for its automated sample pipeline, but the table still shows some numeric differences.
```
caveat:
The evidence supports the paper's reliability claim for its automated sample pipeline, but the table still shows some numeric differences.
```

evidence_text: The paper reports a clear negative correlation between pass rate and required code length, while reporting little apparent dependence of task performance on commit time.
source_excerpt: "We observe a clear negative correlation between pass rate and code length, indicating that tasks involving more lines of code are inherently more challenging for current large models. In contrast, task performance shows minimal dependence on commit time, likely because the task set remains largely unexplored by existing models."
note:
This is a qualitative trend claim in the text; the excerpt does not provide correlation coefficients or significance statistics here.
```
caveat:
This is a qualitative trend claim in the text; the excerpt does not provide correlation coefficients or significance statistics here.
```

evidence_text: The LLM-based classifier for identifying top-level tested objects is reported at 81.03% precision, 89.24% recall, 84.94% F1, and 91.74% accuracy against expert annotations.
source_excerpt: "Domain experts evaluated all 605 import statements in the Lite Set and identified 158 of them as top-level tested objects." Table 9 reports "Precision 81.03%", "Recall 89.24%", "F1 Score 84.94%", and "Accuracy 91.74%".
note:
This evidence concerns the benchmark construction pipeline rather than downstream model solving performance.
```
caveat:
This evidence concerns the benchmark construction pipeline rather than downstream model solving performance.
```

evidence_text: The failure analysis reports NameError as the dominant failure mode for Claude Opus 4.5 and links it to difficulties with cross-file dependency resolution.
source_excerpt: "As shown in Figure 4, the dominance of NameError suggests that current LLMs still struggle with cross-file dependency resolution. When a feature spans multiple files, models often focus on local edits without consistently re-establishing all necessary references, leading to unresolved symbols and frequent name-related failures."
note:
This is an interpretive failure-analysis claim based on observed error-category dominance, not a controlled causal test.
```
caveat:
This is an interpretive failure-analysis claim based on observed error-category dominance, not a controlled causal test.
```

evidence_text: The paper reports that agents often guess or hallucinate interfaces and attributes across files instead of reading files, and associates this behavior with many TypeError and AttributeError failures.
source_excerpt: "We also find that current LLMs exhibit a tendency toward 'laziness'. For example, they often resort to guessing (even hallucinating) the interface or attributes of components defined across files, rather than performing the actual file reading required to retrieve precise prototypes and members. This behavior leads to a considerable number of both TypeError and AttributeError occurrences."
note:
This is a behavioral interpretation from log analysis rather than a quantified controlled experiment in the main text.
```
caveat:
This is a behavioral interpretation from log analysis rather than a quantified controlled experiment in the main text.
```

evidence_text: Among the remaining failures after runtime-crash categories, AssertionError is reported as the most frequent category, which the paper interprets as evidence that many generated solutions execute through to assertion checks.
source_excerpt: "Among the remaining failures, AssertionError becomes the most frequent category. This suggests that a substantial portion of LLM-generated solutions can run to the assertion checkpoints without earlier runtime crashes."
note: The statement combines an observed frequency pattern with an interpretation about solution completeness; the excerpt does not provide raw counts in the captured text.
caveat: The statement combines an observed frequency pattern with an interpretation about solution completeness; the excerpt does not provide raw counts in the captured text.
